data.title: Top biotech scientist Chen Xiaoyuan denies sexual harassment claim, says he will appeal
data.description:
Biomedical scientist Professor Chen Xiaoyuan denies allegations of sexual harassment and says he will appeal against having his tenure terminated by the National University of Singapore (NUS) over an alleged breach of staff conduct policies.
The termination, confirmed to the South China Morning Post by NUS on Wednesday, followed an internal investigation into claims by a female PhD candidate who accused Chen of repeated verbal and physical misconduct since early this year.
Chen broke his silence the following day, along with a series of posts by a group identifying itself as “Chen Xiaoyuan Team” on Chinese social media.


While Chen denied writing the posts, he confirmed his intention to appeal and explore legal avenues.
“I am appealing the termination of my tenure,” Chen told the Post on the evening of December 25. “I deny the complainant’s allegations and I am unable to comment further because I intend to pursue the appeals process and my legal remedies.”
Allegations about Chen’s behaviour circulated on social media before he became the subject of an investigation by NUS that started midyear and included hearings. The university terminated his tenured professorship following the inquiry.
On Thursday, accounts affiliated with the so-called Chen Xiaoyuan Team refuted the sexual harassment allegations on Chinese social media and made claims about the alleged victim. The team of unnamed commenters announced plans to take legal action against her.
Chen, who is originally from Taixing City, Jiangsu province, earned his PhD from the University of Idaho in 1999. In 2009, he became a tenured senior researcher at the National Institute of Biomedical Imaging and Bioengineering, NIH, and directed the Laboratory of Molecular Imaging and Nanomedicine in the United States.
Chen joined the Yong Loo Lin School of Medicine and the school of engineering at NUS in 2020 as the Nasrat Muzayyin Chair Professor with tenure.
He has carried out years of research in nuclear medicine and nanomedicine, published more than 1,000 peer-reviewed papers and was last year elected a Fellow of the National Academy of Sciences, Singapore.

His accuser joined Chen’s lab in March last year. The lab’s website shows a recent team photo of more than 40 members, most of whom are female.
The woman said on social media that she had endured repeated verbal and physical sexual harassment this year. She alleged that many other women in Chen’s lab had experienced similar incidents.
“One weekend in spring 2025, my supervisor grabbed my left hand while dining, praised my good looks and figure, and put his right hand on my thigh,” she wrote.
On June 25, she took her allegations to NUS, filing a complaint of “misconduct”. On October 30, NUS held a related hearing.
After accusing Chen of misconduct, the student transferred to another lab at NUS.
In a response email to her post, NUS alleged: “The various instances of physical contact over several months were unsolicited and unwanted, causing you discomfort, offence and distress.
“The University concluded that termination of his employment was appropriate.”
Chen Xiaoyuan Team responded on Thursday with an open letter and allegations of its own about the woman’s behaviour and professionalism in Chen’s lab, including that she asked Chen to grant her co-authorship on a paper and promote her.
The anonymous commenters said that on June 25, as Chen prepared the woman’s performance review, she accused him of misconduct.
In the open letter, team Chen urged the relevant authorities to intervene in the investigation and hold the woman legally accountable, accusing her of malicious defamation, invasion of privacy and disseminating false information.
The Post has sought comment from the woman, Professor Chen Xiaoyuan and NUS.
The accuser confirmed her personal information and some details regarding her complaint and social media post, but said she was not willing to be interviewed further.


Professor Chen’s NUS email has been deactivated. His reply from a personal email denies responsibility for Chen Xiaoyuan Team social media activity.
“Other people can do whatever but I will not be able to provide any details,” Chen said.
“It’s not me. All the posts are voluntary; they did not go thru me either. I do not have accounts either. Never read though.”
Chen did not disclose where he was corresponding from.
At the time of publication, NUS officials had not responded to inquiries about the public letter from Chen Xiaoyuan Team and had not issued a public statement.

data.title: As the US pursues unilateralism, China faces challenging task as Apec host
data.description:
For the Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation (Apec) forum, which in 2026 will be under China’s leadership for the first time in 12 years, the critical challenge for the year ahead rests at the end of its name – economic cooperation.
Never in its 36-year history has the 21-member grouping’s challenges been so daunting. US President Donald Trump’s unilateralist agenda and assault on multilateral institutions and the principles of multilateral cooperation have threatened Apec’s mission to encourage cooperation across the Asia-Pacific.
As China’s Deputy Foreign Minister Ma Zhaoxu said at Apec’s informal meeting for senior officials in Shenzhen two weeks ago: “Rising protectionism and anti-globalisation trends have created many uncertainties and challenges, placing pressure on economic growth and leaving difficult issues that are hard to address.”
As an ardent promoter of multilateralism and international cooperation on trade and investment, China will undoubtedly use its time as Apec chair to urge cooperation wherever possible. Beijing does the same in other multilateral organisations, not just the World Trade Organization (WTO) but also groupings such as the Brics bloc of developing nations, the Shanghai Cooperation Organisation and its own Belt and Road Initiative.
That said, Apec has distinct leverage. As South Korea’s Foreign Minister Cho Hyun wrote in Global Asia in September, “Apec’s non-binding, consensus-driven design, envisioned by Apec’s founders, fosters candid exchanges that other forums often cannot.”
China’s immediate challenge is obvious. Trump’s tariff war is focused acutely on China and its record US$1 trillion trade surplus. His perception is that China’s recent economic ascent has been at the expense of US manufacturing, so a collaborative Apec agenda will be difficult to define and even more difficult to deliver. China’s modest 2026 priorities of “openness, innovation and cooperation” will be easier said than done.




There will be other challenges, too. Simply getting senior US officials to attend meetings in mainland China is likely to be difficult given the antagonism between Beijing and Washington. Trump’s planned visit to China in April might help, but caution remains. There will be three large clusters of senior officials’ meetings between February and August, as well as at least 10 ministerial meetings ahead of the summit in November, requiring the participation of tens of thousands of Apec officials.
Tensions with Taiwan, Japan and the Philippines mean that many of their officials might be reluctant to travel to mainland China, while at the same time potentially frustrating efforts to build consensus on the challenges facing Asia’s economies. It will be important to watch the pivotal trade ministers’ meeting for both attendance and success in agreeing on a consensus ministerial statement.
Beijing’s leaders will undoubtedly be keen to showcase the country’s progress during the Apec senior officials meetings in Guangzhou (in February), Shanghai (in April) and Dalian (in August), during the ministerial meetings and at the November summit in Shenzhen.
But that urge to showcase the country’s success is set against a backdrop of rising international anxiety over threats of Chinese economic domination. As China’s direct trade with the US has been throttled by Trump’s tariff war, China’s exports are now in search of new markets, foremost across Southeast Asian nations and other Apec members.


Protectionist stirrings are close to the surface in many countries across the region and beyond. China’s Apec officials will have their work cut out for them: they will need to calm anxieties and substantiate the narrative that China is a global leader in multilateral cooperation and the international rules-based order.
Hong Kong faces its own challenges in hosting the Apec finance ministers’ meeting in October. While the city government will be keen to showcase Hong Kong’s prowess as Asia’s most dynamic international finance hub, it must at the same time ensure Hong Kong speaks with its own voice.
As a distinct member of Apec in its own right – also with independent standing at the WTO in Geneva – Hong Kong will need to ensure that other Apec members do not simply see it as a mouthpiece for Beijing. Walking the tightrope of deference to Beijing while creatively asserting its independent contribution to the development of financial policies across the region will be no small challenge.
Because a large proportion of participants in the Apec process will pass through Hong Kong on their way to and from the year’s Apec meetings, the city also has an opportunity to showcase its recovery from the tumult of the past six years. The apex of this activity will be the finance ministers’ meeting, but the traffic of senior officials attending their clusters will be steady throughout the year.


In short, both the Hong Kong government and businesses should brace themselves for a hectic year championing the merits of multilateral cooperation and making clear the harm being done by Trump’s tariffs, protectionism and his efforts to tackle trade reform unilaterally.
There have always been groups dismissive of Apec, but 2026 should be a year to demonstrate the organisation’s abiding merits. The evidence from this year’s Korean leadership suggests the US remains alone in its retreat from multilateral cooperation.
As Cho Hyun wrote: “Most members remain committed to free trade, regional economic integration, multilateral engagement and a rules-based order. Even where disagreements persist, there is a consensus on one essential point: Apec remains indispensable.”

data.title: In pictures: Christmas and New Year’s Eve parties in Hong Kong from the 1970s to 90s
data.description:
Hong Kong has long since embraced the joy of Christmas and New Year’s Eve, with festive gatherings being at the heart of the season, with holiday food, drinks and dancing aplenty.
Through the lens of South China Morning Post photographers, we look back at these celebrations from the 1970s to the 90s – from lively corporate parties to charity events that brought smiles to the disadvantaged.
These moments show how Hong Kong people came together, year after year, to share laughter and the simple pleasure of holiday parties.

data.title: Netflix K-drama Cashero review: Lee Jun-ho can’t save this messy superhero spectacle
data.description:
1.5/5 stars
Lead cast: Lee Jun-ho, Kim Hye-jun, Kim Byung-chul, Kim Hyang-gi
Netflix Korea closes out a big year with the eight-part series Cashero, a lively but garbled medley of social themes, melodrama and genre spectacle.
Lee Jun-ho (Typhoon Family) leads the cast as the hapless Kang Sang-ung, whose grumpy father foists an unwanted superpower upon him, only too glad to finally be rid of it.


The central conceit of the show, and the webtoon on which it is based, revolves around the unusual power that Sang-ung inherits. He has been given superhuman strength, but he can only access it when he has cash on his person – the more he has, the stronger he gets. The problem is that when he uses his superpowers, it drains his cash.
This is quite a pickle for a young man already faced with the daunting prospect of getting on the Korean property ladder. All Sang-ung wants to do is keep his head down and save up for a flat with his accountant girlfriend Kim Min-suk (Kim Hye-jun, A Shop for Killers).
Sang-ung’s father insists that he has to use the power for good. No problem, he just will not use it at all. Formerly a nice young civil servant, Sang-ung now goes out of his way to avoid helping others, but events conspire to keep him off the sidelines until he is forced to become a hero.
Combining contemporary superhero thrills and pressing social themes, the core concept is a compelling one. There are many ways the show could have played with this idea, and early on, it briefly uses it to tease out the natural tension that already exists between personal need and social responsibility.

Sang-ung cannot access his power with a credit card or his savings account, only cold hard cash, so whenever some lands in his lap, the clock starts ticking – can he safely deposit it in Min-suk’s care before it is too late?
Cashero is in such a hurry to become a superhero thriller that it burns through this narrative capital almost immediately when Sang-ung receives a surprise bag of cash from his mother. The walk home proves to be a long and eventful one.
The show tries to scale up from there, but the novelty – and crucially the tension – had already been spent in this early set piece.
The set-up is also ripe for other creative deployments. Disappointingly, the show does not seem interested in exploring them, save for the occasional low-hanging fruit, such as when Min-suk slips an extra 50,000 won (US$34) bill into Sang-ung’s allowance to test his virility in the bedroom.

Kim Hye-jun has become a fixture in big-budget genre fare like Kingdom and Connect, playing assertive female characters.
While Min-suk is marginally more level-headed than Sang-ung, this role plays into familiar local stereotypes of women taking over the household ledger and obsessing over real estate. She is the nagging girlfriend, while Sang-ung gets to be the reluctant hero without making any effort.
Cashero does understand its potential as a comedy, as it expands the superhero pool to Sang-ung’s sidekicks: Byeon Ho-in (Kim Byung-chul), who can move through walls when he drinks alcohol, and Bang Eun-mi (Kim Hyang-gi), whose ability to move objects with her mind is activated by bingeing on snacks.
But again, the comedic properties of these characters are seldom satisfyingly mined. Instead, they stay on standby as Sang-ung’s superhero support network.

A classic example of “Konglish” – Korean portmanteaus of English words – the title combines “cash” and “hero” much the same way that the series mashes up social consciousness and superhero thrills, without finesse.
The first episode of Cashero is far from terrible; alas, the subsequent episodes falter.
As an action thriller, the show is generic, amounting to an underpowered facsimile of superior superhero fare like Moving and The Atypical Family. It even pales in comparison to the already mixed merits of genetic-mutation sci-fi properties such as The Witch and L.U.C.A.: The Beginning.
But by far Cashero’s biggest problem is its clumsy writing. Alongside its failure to capitalise on its compelling concept, the show’s inability to craft a narratively and emotionally coherent story is even more critical.

At one point, Sang-ung is horrified to see civilians fall to their deaths beside villain Jonathan (Lee Chae-min, Bon Appétit, Your Majesty), who eyes him maliciously. The show cuts to Sang-ung arriving home, cueing a lovey-dovey scene of him admiring Min-suk as she sleeps, his trauma quickly cured.
From scene to scene, Cashero never knows what it wants to be.
Assembling a stockpile of VFX-heavy set pieces and disparate emotional beats, then thrusting the resulting heap in front of us, the show seems content to throw its hands up in the air – leaving it up to the audience to figure out the mess for themselves.
Cashero is streaming on Netflix.

data.title: Indonesia in 2025: a year of collision and contrast as Prabowo takes charge
data.description:
Indonesia’s 2025 unfolded as a year of collision and contrast – President Prabowo Subianto asserting his authority, mass protests erupting in the streets, global diplomacy expanding outwards, climate disaster striking at home and a viral moment that turned aura farming into the country’s top cultural export of the year.
Prabowo takes charge
Prabowo entered his first full year in office with a strong mandate and an unusually high level of public goodwill. Over 2025, he moved quickly to consolidate authority and set direction, pairing ambitious domestic pledges with an outward-looking foreign policy and projecting an image of decisiveness after years of coalition-driven politics.
At home, Prabowo sought to brand himself as a hands-on, people-focused leader through highly visible initiatives aimed at welfare and economic security. His flagship free nutritious meals programme for schoolchildren became the clearest expression of that approach, while other moves – including pledges to boost food self-sufficiency, tighten state stewardship of strategic resources and streamline decision-making – reinforced a preference for bold, centralised action.

Not all of it went smoothly. The meals programme ran into food safety lapses and budget pressures, forcing adjustments to funding and roll-out. Elsewhere, market jitters over fiscal discipline, a cabinet reshuffle and the launch of powerful new state investment vehicles raised questions about governance and transparency. The year also brought bouts of street unrest, culminating in major protests that tested the government’s response to public anger and police accountability.
Despite the criticism, Prabowo largely resisted backtracking, framing setbacks as the cost of moving fast and arguing that long-term gains would outweigh early turbulence.
Politically, those challenges did little to derail him. Prabowo closed the year on firm footing, with his approval rating steady at 78 per cent in a November survey, reflecting continued public confidence despite economic pressures and policy criticism. Analysts point to a fragmented opposition, a broad governing coalition that has largely backed his agenda, and a public appetite for stability that has given Prabowo room to recalibrate without paying a heavy political price.
On the international stage, Prabowo moved quickly to raise Indonesia’s profile, emphasising strategic autonomy and a more assertive role in a multipolar world. He positioned Indonesia as an active voice on the war in Gaza, attending international talks and backing ceasefire efforts in line with Jakarta’s long-standing support for Palestinian statehood, an issue that resonates strongly at home. He also held bilateral meetings with major leaders across Asia, the Middle East and beyond, pitching Indonesia as a stable partner open to investment and cooperation.


Dark days of unrest
Public anger surfaced early in the year with the “Indonesia Gelap” (Dark Indonesia) protests, a wave of student-led demonstrations in February and March against spending cuts, rising living costs and fears that Prabowo’s early policy moves would prioritise fiscal discipline and elite interests over social welfare. While largely peaceful, the protests tapped into deeper unease about inequality, governance and the direction of the new administration, setting an early tone of tension between the government and younger Indonesians.
That simmering discontent erupted more violently in late August, when protests spread across major cities over economic grievances and generous perks for lawmakers.
The situation escalated sharply after a 21-year-old motorcycle taxi driver was killed when he was struck by an armoured police vehicle during clashes near the national parliament in Jakarta. Videos of the incident circulated widely online, transforming what had been policy-driven demonstrations into a nationwide outcry over police violence, accountability and the perceived impunity of security forces.
The unrest quickly broadened in scope and intensity, drawing students, gig workers and civil society groups, with some rallies devolving into clashes as police deployed tear gas, water cannon and mass arrests. Although order was eventually restored, the episode laid bare deep public mistrust towards law enforcement and long-standing frustrations over stalled reform.
For Prabowo, the protests became a closely watched test of leadership. He expressed condolences and ordered investigations into the driver’s death, while backing police efforts to restore stability. Rights groups and activists, however, argued the response prioritised control over accountability.

Balancing Brics
Indonesia’s decision to become a full member of Brics – the bloc of emerging economies including Brazil, Russia, India and China – in early January marked a notable step in its foreign policy.
At home, the decision was framed less as a geopolitical pivot than as a statement of independence. Officials stressed that Jakarta was widening its options in a world shaped by intensifying US–China rivalry, not choosing sides. For Prabowo, Brics membership dovetailed with Indonesia’s independent foreign policy doctrine, but with a more confident pitch that cast the country as a player seeking to shape global rules rather than simply follow them.
Prabowo’s bet on Danantara
Danantara emerged in 2025 as one of Prabowo’s most consequential economic initiatives: a new state investment vehicle designed to consolidate major state-owned assets, lift returns and channel them into strategic priorities.
Launched in February, Danantara was pitched as a Temasek-style model for Indonesia – part holding company, part investment fund – with tens of billions of dollars earmarked for sectors ranging from downstream minerals and energy to food security and emerging technologies.
From the outset, the fund also became a test of governance. Supporters argued Danantara could finally impose discipline on Indonesia’s sprawling state-owned sector and attract global capital. Critics warned that concentrating control over assets and dividends in a powerful new entity risked weakening oversight and blurring political and commercial decision-making. To counter those concerns, Danantara unveiled a high-profile advisory line-up and repeatedly promised transparency and professional management.
As the year progressed, Danantara moved from concept to courting partners, exploring overseas investments and joint ventures. That expansion brought fresh controversy, including domestic backlash over potential foreign tie-ups seen as politically sensitive.

Nusantara’s uncertain path
Indonesia’s ambitious plan to build a new capital in East Kalimantan entered a more restrained phase in 2025, with Prabowo paring back the project that had been central to his predecessor Joko Widodo’s legacy.
Early in the year, the government trimmed funding and dialled back rhetoric, reframing Nusantara primarily as a political and administrative capital rather than a near-term commercial hub. The shift marked a clear contrast in tone and ambition, with officials emphasising the relocation of core government functions by 2028 over the creation of a fully fledged new metropolis. Construction continued on key state buildings, but the broader vision was narrowed and expectations reset.
Legal developments added to the uncertainty. A court ruling late in the year curtailing unusually long land-use rights – once marketed as a major incentive for investors – unsettled confidence and forced a rethink of financing assumptions. Supporters argued the decision reinforced legal safeguards and land governance; critics saw it as another blow to already cautious private investment.

Bali’s tourism reset
In 2025, Bali moved decisively to rein in its tourism industry, responding to growing concern that years of unchecked visitor growth were straining infrastructure, eroding culture and testing local patience. The push was framed as a shift towards “quality over quantity” – encouraging visitors to respect local norms and contribute more meaningfully to the economy.
Central to the reset were tighter rules for foreign visitors. Updated guidelines issued by the provincial government set out expectations on dress, conduct at religious sites, traffic compliance and the use of licensed guides and accommodation, with violations risking fines or deportation.
Officials also stepped up action against unlicensed villas and foreign-owned businesses operating illegally, responding to complaints that they undercut local enterprises and drain tax income. While more radical ideas – such as a daily tourist fee or bans on short-term rental platforms – were floated, leaders stopped short of sweeping restrictions, opting instead for tighter regulation.

Disaster and devastation
Indonesia’s year ended in tragedy after a rare cyclone triggered devastating floods and landslides across Sumatra in late November, killing more than 1,000 people and displacing many more. Torrential rains swept through parts of Aceh, North Sumatra and West Sumatra, washing away homes, destroying roads and bridges and leaving entire communities cut off from aid, turning the disaster into one of the country’s deadliest in years.
The immediate humanitarian response stretched emergency services to their limits. Rescuers battled mud, debris and damaged infrastructure to reach survivors, while shortages of food, clean water and medical supplies compounded the crisis. As the scale of destruction became clear, the floods evolved into a national reckoning over preparedness and resilience.
Attention quickly turned to deeper causes. Environmental groups and scientists warned that decades of deforestation, mining and weak land-use enforcement had worsened the impact of extreme rainfall, increasing the risk of landslides and flash floods. Authorities suspended some activities in sensitive areas and promised tighter oversight, while conservationists pointed to severe ecological damage, including the loss of habitat for endangered wildlife.

‘Aura farming’
Indonesia had an unexpected pop-culture moment in 2025 when an 11-year-old boy from Riau turned a traditional riverboat race into a worldwide internet obsession. A short clip of Rayyan Arkan Dikha, filmed standing at the bow of a speeding longboat during the Pacu Jalur festival, went viral after he was seen calmly swaying and posing in traditional dress and mirrored sunglasses.
The clip quickly became an iconic example of “aura farming” – Gen Z internet slang for projecting effortless cool – and within days Rayyan’s understated moves were being copied across social media by athletes, celebrities and even military units far beyond Indonesia. What began as a fleeting clip morphed into a global meme, introducing millions to a centuries-old Sumatran tradition few outside the region had heard of.

data.title: Year in football: expansion, mismatches point to direction Fifa will take ‘beautiful game’
data.description:
Bigger does not always mean better but try telling that to those who govern the world’s most popular sport as they reflect on a jam-packed year and begin the countdown to a supersized World Cup that will dwarf previous tournaments.
World football’s governing body, Fifa – not to mention continental confederations, national leagues and broadcasters – will argue that the appetite for the “beautiful game” is insatiable and will point to the past 12 months as evidence.
Fifa described its expanded 32-team Club World Cup as a “roaring success” with 2.4 million fans attending the summer tournament in the United States, including 81,000 for the final in which Chelsea beat Paris St Germain.
The fact Auckland City FC lost 10-0 to Bayern Munich and 6-0 to Benfica while Al-Ain suffered 5-0 and 6-0 defeats raised questions about quality control.
Such mismatches, however, will litter the World Cup.

A record 48 nations – up from 32 in Qatar in 2022 – will descend on the United States, Canada and Mexico for a 104-match tournament that will test the endurance of players, supporters and media to the absolute limit.
Player welfare has already become a flashpoint, with FIFPro warning of rising injury rates and burnout amid an ever-expanding calendar.
Once the likes of Cabo Verde, Curacao, Jordan and Uzbekistan have had their moments in the sun, the World Cup will more than likely arrive at its business end with the usual suspects – holders Argentina, France, Brazil, Spain, England and Germany – scrapping for the coveted crown.
Messi and Ronaldo could meet in last dance
Television commentators will require copious notes on some unfamiliar names, but two players will need no introduction.
Argentina’s Lionel Messi and Portugal’s Cristiano Ronaldo, the two dominant players of the 21st century, have never faced each other in their five previous World Cups but, if results pan out as expected, could meet for a last dance in a Kansas City quarter-final, by which time Ronaldo would be 41 and Messi 39.

Neither showed any sign of losing their hunger in 2025, with Ronaldo banging in the goals for Al-Nassr and helping Portugal to the Uefa Nations League title, while Messi won the MLS Cup with Inter Miami plus the MVP award and MLS Golden Boot.
With the pair away from football’s European heartland, other talents have flourished.
None more so than Ballon d’Or and Fifa Player of the Year winner Ousmane Dembélé, whose goals propelled Paris St Germain to a treble, including their first Uefa Champions League title.
PSG finally deliver
Dembele scored 33 goals in 49 appearances in all competitions and made two assists as PSG finally delivered for their Qatari owners by thrashing Inter Milan 5-0 in the final.
He edged some top-notch competition including Lamine Yamal, who played a key role in Barcelona’s 28th Spanish title, and Egypt’s Mohamed Salah, whose 47 goal involvements (29 goals and 18 assists) powered Liverpool to the Premier League title in a dream first season for Dutch coach Arne Slot.
England captain Harry Kane was only 13th on the list despite finishing as the Bundesliga’s top scorer for the second successive season as Bayern Munich claimed the title.
Barca’s Aitana Bonmati secured a third consecutive Ballon d’Or Féminin to underline Spain’s depth at the top of the women’s game, although she suffered heartbreak as her country lost the Euro 2025 final on penalties to England.

That tournament continued the surge of the women’s game with record-breaking attendances, while Fifa confirmed plans for an expanded Women’s Club World Cup starting in 2027.
Uefa’s expanded men’s Champions League with its 36-team group phase won over the sceptics, with 27 clubs still having something on the line in the eighth and final round of matches.
Exciting as that was, the format played into the hands of the big clubs – none more so than PSG, who lost three of their first five games and finished 15th before catching fire in the knockout phase.
Six English clubs qualified for the 2025-26 Champions League – proof, if any were needed, that the Premier League is indeed in a league of its own. Its 20 clubs spent a combined £3 billion (US$4.01 billion) in the summer transfer window – more than the Bundesliga, La Liga, Ligue 1 and Serie A clubs combined.
Technology also evolved in 2025 with Fifa rolling out accelerated semi-automated offside detection while next year’s World Cup will be the most hi-tech ever, with even the new Trionda ball fitted with an AI chip designed to help match officials get decisions right.

data.title: Beijing municipality eases home purchase curbs to shore up sluggish market
data.description:
The Beijing municipal government has further relaxed home buying curbs by lowering the threshold for individual income tax payments and by allowing multi-child families to purchase extra properties.
The move was seen as a precursor to more national supportive policies expected in 2026 aimed at shoring up the sluggish property market, an industry expert said.
China aims to stabilise the nation’s property market as part of its 15th Five-Year Plan, which kicks off in 2026, according to a national conference held in Beijing earlier this week.
The policies rolled out in the national capital of Beijing were also seen as a benchmark for the rest of the country, said Liu Jing, a professor of accounting and finance, specialising in real estate, at Cheung Kong Graduate School of Business in Beijing.
Non-Beijing hukou residents, or those without official residency, are now eligible to buy homes within the Fifth Ring Road, a major expressway around the city, after completing at least two consecutive years of local income tax or social security payments, down from a three-year requirement previously.
The eligibility threshold for homes purchased outside the Fifth Ring Road has been lowered to one year of tax payments, down from two, according to guidelines issued on Wednesday by the Beijing Municipal Commission of Housing and Urban-Rural Development and other authorities.

“This supportive policy will deliver positive effects, yet its direct impact remains limited,” Liu said. “At its core, the property market hinges on the dynamics of supply and demand. Stabilising the property market is a crucial measure to anchor economic fundamentals, and I expect more robust measures will be rolled out.”
Liu added that policies such as cutting interest rates could have a tangible impact on the property market, but such policies required trillions of yuan in financial support.
China’s property sector, once a pillar of economic growth, has been on a downward trajectory since the second half of 2021. Sluggish home sales and declining prices have dented consumer confidence and taken a toll on homeowners.
The new policy also allows families with two children or more to purchase an extra home in downtown Beijing. Specifically, Beijing-hukou households can own up to three properties within the Fifth Ring Road, while non-Beijing-hukou families with at least two consecutive years of local tax payments can buy two homes in the area.
In Beijing, transaction volumes of pre-owned units larger than 140 square metres (1,507 sq feet) have maintained their growth. Four-bedroom second-hand properties outside the North Fifth Ring Road have seen a growing share of transactions, according to the Beijing Municipal Commission of Housing.

Data from Centaline Property’s research institute shows that about 80 per cent of Beijing’s new residential property transactions are concentrated in areas outside the Fifth Ring Road, which are also core districts for second-hand home deals.
“As the policy takes effect, rigid demand for new homes outside the Fifth Ring Road is expected to drive the initial pickup in visitor footfall and transaction volumes,” said Zhang Dawei, chief analyst of Centaline Properties in Beijing.
“The new policy is expected to reinforce this trend and accelerate destocking of improvement-oriented residential properties within the Fifth Ring Road,” Zhang said.
In addition, second-hand homebuyers using loans ‍from China’s housing provident fund will only have to make a minimum down payment of 25 per cent, down from 30 per cent.
In the first three quarters of 2025, Beijing’s new residential property sales volume totalled 4.92 million square metres, down 8.2 per cent year on year, while pure commercial residential unit sales reached 3.47 million square metres, up 20.1 per cent on a yearly basis, according to the Beijing Municipal Bureau of Statistics.
Since Beijing’s new policy was rolled out, there has been little change in the volume of inquiries, according to a representative at a real estate agency in Beijing’s Chaoyang district, who requested anonymity.
The agent added that a wait-and-see sentiment typically sets in after a new policy launch, and that the downward trend in the market was unlikely to be easily reversed by policy measures. While real demand would still translate into home purchases, the policies would do little to draw investors into the market.
Another property agent in Beijing’s Xicheng district said some potential buyers have viewed properties since the new policy was introduced, but they are also taking a wait-and-see approach.
“Homebuyers are no longer making panic purchases. Instead, they are approaching the market with greater rationality,” the agent said.

data.title: In South Korea, vouchers to curb county’s falling population draw new residents
data.description:
A novel approach by South Korea to stop its rural areas from dying by paying people to live there appears to be working.
Okcheon in North Chungcheong province saw its population grow by almost 1,000 in just over two weeks since it became a participant in the state-backed programme earlier this month, according to The Korea Herald newspaper.
The farming community was officially designated a “population decline area” in 2021. By the following year, its population fell to below the 50,000 threshold, making it a top contender for the “rural basic income” programme.
Nine other communities were selected, including Yeoncheon in Gyeonggi province, Chungseon in Gangwon province and Namhae in South Gyeongsang province.
Under the scheme, residents will receive 150,000 won (US$104) in local vouchers per month for two years. No proof of employment is necessary.
For a four-person household, the benefit will total 14.4 million won over two years – a significant sum given that annual income in farming communities has stagnated at about 10 million won for the past 30 years, according to a 2023 paper by the National Agricultural Cooperative Federation.

The stipend proved irresistible at a time when a widening wage gap is forcing young people from regional areas to flock to Seoul. Those who moved between 2022 and 2023 saw their average annual income rise by 22.8 per cent, The Korea Herald reported, citing a Ministry of Data and Statistics survey released earlier this month.
From December 3, when Okcheon was named one of 10 pilot regions for the programme, to December 19, 976 people made the county their registered home, according to Yonhap news agency.
This pushed the number of new residents for the year up to 1,038, a 10-fold increase from the same period last year. On average, about 70 people a day registered Okcheon as their new address over the period this December.
Okcheon’s population, which was 48,427 at the end of last December, has risen to 49,227 as of December 22.
“The number of people moving in began to increase last month, and since the announcement, inquiries and applications have increased rapidly,” an Okcheon official told the Maeil Business Newspaper. “The moving-in window is expected to be crowded for the time being.”
With the rapid population growth, Okcheon is facing new headaches, the biggest of which is cost.


The central government has pledged to cover 40 per cent of the voucher costs, but provincial and local governments will have to cover the rest.
Okcheon will have to find about 53 billion won in funding in the next two years – about 30 per cent of the county’s entire budget. More than 3.5 billion won is needed to cover this month’s new residents alone.
The county council is now planning ways to identify fake residents who only change addresses without moving in. Measures against these “paper residencies” include on-site checks and oversight by a committee of village heads.
“We see the increase as a clear expectation effect from the basic income,” a county official told The Korea Herald. “But our focus is making sure it leads to real settlement, not temporary registration.”

data.title: Malaysia’s Najib found guilty on abuse of power charge in final 1MDB case
data.description:
Malaysia’s jailed former prime minister Najib Razak was found guilty on one count of abuse of power in the final criminal case linked to the multibillion-dollar 1Malaysia Development Berhad (1MDB) scandal, following a seven-year trial.
Justice Collin Lawrence Sequerah is still reading the remaining verdicts.
Prosecutors say that Najib abused his position to siphon 2.3 billion ringgit (US$490 million) from the scandal-hit state fund.
In total, Najib, 72, faces four charges of abusing his position to obtain the monies in gratification linked to 1MDB funds, along with 21 additional counts of money laundering.
The decision is being delivered at the Palace of Justice in Putrajaya.
The former leader could face maximum jail terms of between 15 and 20 years on each charge, as well as a fine of up to five times the value of the alleged misappropriations.
Najib is currently held at Kajang Prison, where he is more than halfway through a reduced six-year sentence for a conviction in a separate case involving a former 1MDB subsidiary.
Dressed in a navy suit, Najib sat in the dock on Friday, mostly looking down, with his children and wife, Rosmah Mansor, seated behind him.

Throughout the trial that stretched over seven years, Najib’s defence team, led by senior lawyer Muhammad Shafee Abdullah, denied any of his client’s wrongdoing and maintained that the funds were legitimate political donations from Saudi Arabia.
His lawyers said Najib had been deceived by fugitive financier Low Taek Jho, also known as Jho Low, whom they described as the “main architect” of the financial scandal.
But the court on Friday said the evidence showed an unmistakable bond between Najib and Low.
“The evidence clearly points to the fact that this was no coincidence but was evident of a relationship in which Jho Low operated as a proxy or agent of [Najib] with regard to the running of the affairs of 1MDB,” Judge Collin Lawrence Sequerah said.
“The attempt where Najib to distance [himself] from Jho Low rang hollow in the light of the overwhelming evidence of these prosecution witnesses.”
Sequerah also said that Najib’s failure to report Low amounted to condonation, given the power he wielded at the time.
“[Najib] was no country bumpkin but was possessed of not only an impeccable family and political pedigree but of superior intelligence given his academic background and his previous experience in politics and in the administration, not only regionally but also on a national level,” the judge said.
A career politician, Najib entered frontline politics at the age of 23 after returning from studies in London to contest the Pekan parliamentary seat in his home state of Pahang following the death of his father, Abdul Razak Hussein, Malaysia’s highly respected second prime minister, in 1976.
He became a deputy minister at 25, later served as Pahang chief minister and defence minister before assuming office as Malaysia’s prime minister in 2009.
Low, who remains at large, is wanted by authorities in Malaysia, the United States and Singapore.

US investigators have estimated that more than US$4.5 billion was siphoned from 1MDB through a web of fraudulent shell companies involving corrupt officials and their associates. More than US$1 billion allegedly flowed into bank accounts owned by Najib, who helped to establish the fund in 2009.
Stolen money was traced to luxury real estate in New York and Los Angeles, a superyacht once owned by Low and high-value artworks by artists including Monet and Van Gogh.
At the height of the scandal, police and anti-corruption officers raided properties linked to Najib and his family, seizing luxury handbags, jewellery and watches worth hundreds of millions of dollars and cash in foreign currencies.
The looted billions were also used to finance The Wolf of Wall Street, starring Leonardo DiCaprio, who later returned artworks and other gifts tied to the case after US authorities launched asset recovery proceedings.
In October, Malaysia’s government said more than 20 billion ringgit was recovered through settlements with financial institutions, including a multibillion-dollar agreement with Goldman Sachs, which in 2020 admitted to bribing officials to secure bond deals for 1MDB.
The corruption scandal erupted in 2015 at the height of Najib’s premiership after investigative reports revealed billions of dollars missing from the state fund.
The saga ultimately contributed to the collapse of his government in a shock election defeat in 2018, ending six decades of rule by the Barisan Nasional coalition.
The scale of the alleged losses outraged Malaysians and fuelled public anger over corruption and elite excess at a time of rising living costs.


Last October, Najib issued a rare public apology over the scandal, saying it pained him “every day” that the 1MDB debacle occurred on his watch as prime minister and finance minister, though he denied direct responsibility.
“I deeply regret what has taken place at SRC and 1MDB,” he said in a statement. “However, being held legally responsible for things that I did not initiate or knowingly enable is unfair to me and I hope and pray that the judicial process will, in the end, prove my innocence.”
Najib was found guilty of corruption and money laundering in 2020 in a separate case involving funds from SRC International, a 1MDB unit. Two years later, he became the first Malaysian prime minister to be jailed after exhausting all appeals.
Earlier this week, a Malaysian court rejected Najib’s house arrest bid, saying a supposed addendum by former king Sultan Abdullah Ahmad Shah had no legal force as it was not deliberated by the Pardons Board.
Najib, who has been imprisoned since 2022, had his original 12-year jail sentence halved last year following a partial royal pardon.

data.title: Where to ring in 2026 in Hong Kong, from the Mandarin Oriental and Kimpton, to jazz at Zuma
data.description:
It’s been a long and eventful 2025 – and whether you’re ready to welcome 2026 with open arms, or just welcome an excuse to let your hair down, we’ve rounded up a selection of some of Hong Kong’s best nightlife venues for ringing in the New Year.
The Aubrey: After Dark New Year’s Eve Countdown Party

The Mandarin Oriental’s upscale cocktail and izakaya concept brings us a special edition of its Aubrey After Dark parties this December 31. Curated by prominent local nightlife figures Marina Bullivant and Andrew Bull, the countdown party will feature headline sets by DJs Willer and Belloy.
Where: 25/F, Mandarin Oriental, 5 Connaught Road Central
Price: HK$988 (between 10pm and midnight), HK$500 (after midnight)
Terrible Baby: Back to the Future

Over on Kowloon side, Eaton HK and Terrible Baby are doubling down on the New Year’s temporal theme. From 9pm to 2am, Terrible Baby is putting the night in the hands of DJs Fergus, Tedman Lee and Vegan Chicken, in addition to four-piece band Misty Blue Banana.
Where: 4/F, Eaton HK, 380 Nathan Road, Jordan
Price: HK$550 (early bird, until December 28), HK$630
Gossip: The Last Secret of the Year

Socio’s sister venue and nightlife concept promises one last secret and reveal for the year. Starting from 11pm, Gossip’s New Year’s Eve party will feature an exclusive show by DJ Drako as he spins his signature blend of Korean mash-ups and hip-hop.
Where: 3/F, Foco, 48 Cochrane Street, Central
Price: HK$350, including one standard drink
Swim Club: New Year’s Eve Hollywood Sock Hop

If it’s a fresh view of Victoria Harbour you’re looking for to ring in 2026, look no further than Swim Club at the Kimpton. Specially for the occasion, the venue promises “a rooftop scene of neon gloss and retro cool”, complete with gliding rollergirls and rockabilly beats from live DJs. Easy bites like tacos, sliders and grilled skewers will keep guests fuelled alongside free-flow cocktails, beers, wine and, of course, champagne.
Where: Kimpton Tsim Sha Tsui, 11 Middle Road, Tsim Sha Tsui
Price: HK$888 (early bird), HK$1,288
The Trilogy: Golden Countdown

The triple-threat nightlife concept – comprising nightclub Keyz, rooftop bar Wav and jazz bar Ella – atop H Code is pulling out all the stops to finish 2025 with a bang. No surface of the rooftop concept will be without gold – from platters to confetti – as resident DJ Anil Ahuja takes to the decks, to be joined by a surprise guest DJ later in the night.
Where: 26/F, H Code, 45 Pottinger Street, Central
Price: HK$338 (online), HK$388 (at the door)
Zuma: New Year’s Eve Countdown Party

Zuma will be blue turning grey (full marks to those who recognise the jazz reference) as the upscale Japanese concept offers a Yashoku night brunch (from 7.30pm, at HK$1,980), before transforming into a jazz club featuring sets by a live band and Zuma’s resident DJ – all enhanced by free-flow Ruinart champagne.
Where: 5-6/F, Landmark Atrium, 15 Queen’s Road Central
Price: HK$1,080
